Key Takeaways:

  • TEM has the best revenue growth which is almost 2x the next highest.

  • TEM has negative net income so it cannot be compared. The company with the best NI growth was ICLR which was also around 2x the next highest.

  • RVTY has the best current ratio with TEM in 4th place.

  • ICLR has the highest cash from operations with ILMN in a close 2nd. TEM is in last place with a negative cash flow from operations.

From a financial health perspective, we see that the picture for TEM is iffy. Other than the highest revenue growth, they do not stand out in any of the other metrics and are one of the 2 names with negative earnings. We question whether TEM’s high valuation can be fully explained by their high growth given that we see peers with better valuations and positive incomes.

Risks

TEM is overvalued compared to peers

As we saw, TEM has a significantly higher valuation than most peers across all the metrics we looked at. Despite its great recent price performance, TEM looks to be likely overvalued based on this analysis. One explanation for the high valuation could be the high revenue growth. However, with continual negative earnings and operating cash flows it is hard to justify the valuation based on revenue growth alone, especially considering that TEM has the lowest revenue out of the group of peers.

The firm remains unprofitable

Despite all the revenue growth and hype around this firm amidst the AI wave, the firm remains unprofitable. Now that they are public, investors will soon desire profitability from them, which is a critical metric and one that they must grow into. We understand that this firm is still young and they are operating in a cutting-edge field, but steps towards profitability are something that we hope the company can show investors over the course of the next couple of quarters.

Conclusion

TEM is an innovative AI player in the healthcare space that has a lot of potential if they can execute and deliver upon their research and testing goals. They have a positive flywheel of data, patients, and insights, and their latest acquisition seems aligned with their long-term goals and vision. We believe that TEM could be a future leader in healthcare AI.

However, given the high relative valuation, negative income and low ranking against peers on sustainability metrics, we do not believe that TEM is a good buy at the moment. If you are looking to get exposure to the industry, other options like ICLR may be worth looking into further. We will keep an eye on TEM and believe that if they can start moving towards profitability, they may be a good purchase in the future. For now, it is a sell.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery And Paramount Merger Might Have Only One Winner
data.description:
After years of essentially insisting that the streaming revolution wasn’t happening, the past two years have seen a flurry of activity as the incumbents race to see off the threat of encroaching tech giants. The latest news comes from Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (NASDAQ:WBD) potentially being on the cusp of receiving a majority-cash acquisition offer from Paramount Skydance Corporation (NASDAQ:PSKY) - itself newly christened only a month ago from the merger of legacy Paramount Global with billionaire Ellison’s Skydance.

I need to preface this article by saying how much we still don’t know. And yes, I know I’ve been saying that a lot lately; the ink is barely dry on the three-way dance between SpaceX, EchoStar (SATS) and AT&T (T) and we are already seeing another major shakeup in the TMT (Tech, Media and Telecom) space, the lines between which continue to blur. Investors should continue to do their own due diligence as news continues to trickle out, which I’m sure it will in the days to come.

Another Major TMT Transaction Taking Shape

Still, I wanted to write now, because what we already know, little as that is, is already moving the markets. The news first: The Wall Street Journal has broken the story that an offer is being prepared at David Ellison’s new company, Paramount Skydance, to largely take out Warner Discovery’s current shareholders and merge two of the Big Five studios in Hollywood.

The market reaction was instantaneous. As of this writing, Warner Discovery is up a staggering 42% on the day; Paramount is up a smaller but respectable 16%. That’s right, both stocks have risen on the news of a buyout being planned; as we all know, that is by no means assured when a major acquisition is afoot. Still, the market seems to feel that for Warner, in particular, which just saw Amazon (AMZN) and Comcast (CMCSA) elbow their way into Warner's prized NBA broadcast slot, this is a deliverance of sorts.

Author History And Interests

Mergers are always a little hard for me to write about because I have to try to separate the two hats that I wear; I went to school for economics, but now I write investment analysis. Usually, the two complement one another nicely, but mergers always strain the marriage inside my head; mergers rarely end up being good for both consumers and investors.

To put my cards on the table, as an economist, I have written both on Seeking Alpha and elsewhere about my views on the current state of antitrust enforcement in the United States, to wit, that it is far too lax. Were I sitting in judgment of most of the mergers that have been proposed in the United States over the last 20 years, I would have blocked the vast majority of them. Sometimes they work out for investors, sometimes they don’t; in my view, the consumer benefits of almost all of them have been sorely lacking.

Nevertheless, as an investor, I recognize there is often money to be made by playing mergers correctly. And the first thing to recognize, of course, is that the government’s view of mergers is very different than mine. That is why I am not convinced that this deal would face the substantial antitrust scrutiny that some expect it would. If the deal can be sealed, I expect that the DOJ would bless it, as would the courts.

America's Fading Echo Of Anti-Trust

Strictly speaking, it shouldn’t. Paramount and Warner are both two of the top five studios in Hollywood, which means that any combination between them necessarily produces an adverse (to the consumer) move in the HHI5 (Herfindahl-Hirschman Index of the top five firms) that most economists use to measure oligopolistic tendencies in a market.

I agree that should sway the DOJ, and a court if it got that far, but I doubt that it will. In the first place, the index is only as good as the base it uses; in order to measure the market share of the top five firms, you first need to define which firms are inside and outside the industry being measured. Arguments will doubtless be made that any or all of Amazon, Apple (AAPL) and Netflix (NFLX) should be considered competitors now, and that therefore Paramount and Warner don’t really represent any sort of oligopolistic threat by combining.

In the second place, the merger, while in my view highly anticompetitive, is no more so than others that have been approved by the Trump Administration, both the first and second. The Sprint - T-Mobile (TMUS) merger, for example, allowed two of the top four competitors to merge in an industry even more highly concentrated than traditional television production. The recent EchoStar deals mean the promised replacement fourth carrier will never materialize, permanently reducing American wireless to a triopoly - and the Trump Administration signed off on that, too.

Two Declining Stocks On A Rebound

The only real stumbling block to this deal, therefore, is likely to be internal; in order for it to close, Warner and Paramount have to agree on a price. And this is where I do have some concerns.

Before the explosion in value that this report produced, Warner stock was trading at barely half what it was when its last merger closed three years ago. Paramount, meanwhile, was down an even more staggering 60% over the same time frame. Were this an all-stock deal, the two companies could probably come to an agreement quickly, being in the same boat, but the report says this is principally a cash transaction.

That’s actually not so surprising. Ellison is the majority- but not sole-owner of Paramount, and having just secured majority control of the company, he is presumably not eager to dilute himself back to minority status - though he may need to agree to go at least a little below 50% in order to seal this deal.

Warner Is Now Selling Itself Twice

But even then, he needs to offer Warner shareholders enough cash to seal the deal. And the timing of this offer makes that a little more difficult. Warner Discovery, some may recall, is right in the middle of a separate strategic transaction to split itself into two separate companies, its linear cable networks in one and its content production and streaming capabilities in the other.

While linear networks have been the source of almost all of Warner Discovery’s positive free cash flow since it became Warner Discovery, I’ve explained before that it owes largely to the sequential timing of Warner’s sports rights step-ups and renewals. In the long term, the linear networks segment is almost certainly doomed to see free cash flow collapse as cord-cutting continues; the long-term potential of the company has always been in its studio and streaming side.

That is precisely what lies at the core of the upcoming spinoff. As I’ve written before, when it’s over, Warner’s bondholders will be left with little more than the linear network shell of the old company, while its streaming/studio beating heart will be beyond their reach. That matters because while linear does have value, its value alone, without streaming/studio, is probably less than the debt that will remain attached to it. In other words, the equity value of linear post spinoff may well be negative.

The spinoff thus reduces bondholders collateral and recovery in what would probably at some point be a liquidation/distressed equity conversion on the linear side, while substantially boosting the value of the Warner common stock by essentially redirecting cash flow from bond interest and principal payments to share dividends and buybacks. Indeed, Warner stock rose 15% after the restructuring was announced, and many industry watchers stated explicitly that was their foundational assumption in bidding up the price.

Crunching The Numbers

This puts the Paramount offer in a somewhat unusual posture. The spinoff is not yet complete, so Paramount is buying both streaming and linear here. Meaning the asset it is buying comes with all the debt. But in order to get Warner’s shareholders on board, it probably needs to offer them what their stock would be worth if the debt was not attached to it. Effectively, if Paramount buys now, there is a good probability that it will need to pay twice for the same assets, once to bondholders and once to shareholders.

How much would that be? After the debt exchange Warner recently completed, there is roughly $35 billion of debt on the company, while Streaming and Studios could realistically hope to return to the $2.5 billion of profit that HBO alone was generating prior to diving into the streaming wars in earnest. At a 25 P/E multiple, that would come to $62.5 billion of equity value.

There might be a little wiggle room here. The separation, while on track to close early next year, is not necessarily going as well as Warner probably hoped when they set out. The idea was to take the best assets and leave the debt behind. But under the terms of the final agreement, Discovery Global, as the new linear networks entity was to be called, would have retained 20% ownership of Warner Brothers, and some debt may have followed the streaming and studios to their new home. That means that the upside of the separation had already been ratcheted down somewhat.

If we assign 20% of Warner’s existing debt to the S&S side and deduct the 20% stake of Discovery Global from our calculations, we get roughly $44.4 billion, which pencils out to roughly $24 per share. If I’m a Warner shareholder, I would want that amount, minimum, in order to do this deal.

The Logic Of The Deal

Even at that (slightly) reduced price, I worry about the deal’s impact on Paramount’s ability to extract value from the combined company. Overpaying in a merger is exactly what crippled this iteration’s Warner's ability to invest in the business. Combined with the debt assumed, Paramount would be paying an EV of close to $80 billion at a time when its own EV is barely $30 billion and most of the linear assets it would be acquiring are dying.

There is, of course, one way the deal could pay off, and it brings me back to the point I was making at the opening about mergers. The plan, almost certainly, is that management does in fact believe that the deal will reduce the competitive intensity of the industry significantly. Which is bad for consumers, but good for investors; it means scope would be opened for substantial increases in pricing. While I have no doubt the companies will also look for synergies in their operations - so like most mergers, the deal is bad for employees, as well - the real value catalyst here is almost certainly going to be simply squeezing consumers for higher prices and lower quality.

Indeed, some are not even really bothering to hide that fact. The very day before the news leaked, Warner CEO David Zaslav was at the Goldman Sachs Communacopia industry conference, and stated bluntly that “there [are] too many players,” and a lot of them had “decided to drop price aggressively.” He spoke almost nostalgically of the days that “consumers in America were paying twice as much 10 years ago for content.”

Speaking as an economist again, I would call that free-market competition, and of course, pressuring producers to offer better prices and quality to consumers is exactly the result it is supposed to produce. But the TV industry in America has gotten used to the luxurious cash flow of the cable monopoly, and this merger will allow them to begin to resurrect that pricing power.

Paramount's Debt Hangover

Could it produce so much pricing power that Paramount could write an $80 billion check and still come out ahead on the deal? I think that’s harder to say. Even this deal would not completely turn back the clock to the good old days; Netflix’s streaming crown to one side, we haven’t even discussed the ongoing penetration of Google’s (GOOG, GOOGL) YouTube, which consistently sits at number one on Nielsen’s monthly Gauge reports.

And the ongoing leverage shift to the sports leagues and away from broadcasters means that even if consumers go back to paying the old prices, Paramount still might not get its old share of them. I also continue to believe - though admittedly it has been an argument few other analysts have credited, thus far - that there is a real danger of a paradigm shift in advertising, as well, which could eat away at TV margins even if subscription fees go sky-high again.

Altogether, if I were Paramount, I would not want to pay over $50 billion at the very most. And it’s why I am still not entirely sure this deal happens, even with a supportive President Trump and the lure of a renewed ability to squeeze consumers. The maximum price I would be willing to offer if I were on the Paramount side of the table is lower than the minimum price I would accept if sitting on the Warner side.

The Core Flaw

The core flaw in this deal is that it comes just as Warner was about to complete a spinoff that would free it from most of its debt burdens - albeit at bondholder expense. A purchase now effectively bails out those bondholders and puts their entire debt load back onto the new company’s balance sheet. There is no getting around that, legally, as long as Paramount seeks to buy in advance of the spinoff’s completion.

While I don’t deny that this deal would indeed substantially increase producers pricing power over consumers, it's less clear to me if any of the benefits of that would be left over for the new Paramount after Warner’s shareholders and bondholders had each taken their cut.

It reinforces, in many ways, my growing concern about Paramount’s new direction under Larry Ellison’s leadership. He is showing a frightening new tendency to overpay on whatever assets are available on the market at any given time; his last major deal for UFC rights showed much the same approach. While mergers absolutely do help businesses gain more power over consumers, there is a price at which they cease to be value generators for the acquirer.

Investment Summary

I am not necessarily surprised that Paramount wants to buy Warner Brothers. I am a little surprised that they want to buy it now, before the linear spinoff has been completed. That could require effectively overpaying to the tune of most of the company’s debt load.

If the deal goes through, I will expect to see Paramount sag under new concerns about a heavy debt load and overpay, much as Zaslav’s company has the past three years. A failure to close, however, would leave existing Warner Discovery spinoff plans intact, which would still leave some upside from even these higher levels since my $24 calculation is itself based on that spinoff, not the merger.

I am getting more and more worried about Paramount, but since I am not sure this deal actually happens, I am going to maintain an (increasingly weak) Buy on it. Meanwhile, I am putting Warner at Buy as well, since I think the spinoff unlocks value - at bondholder expense - regardless of whether it closes a deal with Paramount or not.

data.title: Holding Phreesia Despite Initial Post-Earnings Disappointment
data.description:
Phreesia, Inc. (NYSE:PHR) provides software to healthcare providers for managing the patient experience through activities like scheduling, registration, payments, education, and clinical support. Back in March, I made the case for PHR as a stock to buy that would be largely immune from the tariff-driven turmoil and chaos at the time. PHR eventually broke out as I expected. However, a sharp price run-up following the breakout into Q2 FY 2026 earnings set a high bar of expectations that Phreesia failed to reach.

At the time of writing, PHR has fallen four straight days, including PHR's first negative post-earnings response since June 2024 and a full reversal of its breakout. The stock is now breaking down below price support at the 200-day moving average (DMA) (the blue line below). However, I continue to hold as the investment case remains the same for PHR. I will add shares if PHR manages to recover 200 DMA support.

An Achievement with An Asterisk

The biggest earnings news for PHR came with positive net income for the first time in Phreesia’s history. That news should have reassured investors and kept the stock aloft. However, the earnings presentation includes an asterisk on the positive net income: “Net income was generated in Q2 FY26 due to a deferred tax benefit recognized as a discrete item in the quarter.” This one-time benefit could throw just enough doubt on the significance of achieving positive net income. However, fortunately the trend shows consistent quarterly improvements. The current income improvements have mainly come from absolute reductions in spending on R&D, sales and marketing, and G&A. Each of these categories has declined as a percentage of revenue since FY 2023 (FY 2022 for G&A). The cost of revenue also decreased from 14.8% to 15.8%.

Cash Flow

The increasing income trend has translated to improvements in free cash flow margin. This uptrend keeps me interested in PHR. However, I will next monitor for any changes in the health of this trend after the company’s largest acquisition in its history.

Acquisition

During the earnings conference call, Phreesia fielded a lot of questions about its acquisition of AccessOne, a provider of financing solutions for healthcare receivables. Phreesia made the announcement alongside its earnings report. The news seemed to blindside the market a bit, given the selling pressure on PHR and all the critical, probing questions. With just $98M in cash on the balance sheet, the company had to take a “new fully committed bridge loan facility” to make this deal happen at the $160M cash price. In effect, this spending crowded out the news of the caveated achievement of reaching positive net income. Still, Phreesia paid just 4.6x the annual revenue AccessOne will reportedly bring to the table. Moreover, Phreesia estimated the deal expanded its TAM by $14B (to a $24B total). These numbers clearly did not assuage investors or analysts.

One concerned analyst asked about the financing risk that Phreesia takes on from AccessOne. Management reassured that the provider and AccessOne’s partner, PNC Bank, take on the financing risk. This question was particularly important given Phreesia just completed its shutdown of its clearinghouse client relationship, which was part of its payment processing. At the time, the company did not explain the accelerated timeline for that shutdown, but perhaps it was to make room for the AccessOne deal (just my speculation).

Business Momentum

I am attracted to Phreesia because it develops a positive and valuable end-to-end patient experience. Healthcare providers are aggressively pursuing improvements in customer satisfaction. For example, Keri McCrensky, VP for Digital Transformation in Healthcare at XL, claimed recently that healthcare providers are trying to achieve 10-20% improvements in the patient experience (interview in "Running an AI Transformation Initiative," DataCamp, September 11, 2025). Phreesia can be a key component in such initiatives.

The company has impressive reach, facilitating about 170M patient visits in 2025, processing over $4B in patient payments in the last 12 months, along with over 2000 employees (called "Phreesians") across the U.S. and India. Phreesia's provider customers include hospitals, health systems, and primary care groups. The company also lays claim to all of the top 10 pharma companies as customers. Phreesia has a solid customer base to execute its broad-based strategy. The chart below explains the company's revenue model that provides the visibility for setting and meeting guidance targets.

Phreesia uses a direct sales model to earn new customers. The land and expand model includes growing with existing customers across locations, increasing usage of the product and services, and upselling new products. Phreesia's line-up of products is extensive. Phreesia highlighted its latest innovations in its quarterly shareholder letter:

  • Phreesia VoiceAI: Answers, triages, and routes patient calls in multiple languages using conversational AI and real-time workflow orchestration. Benefits include reduced wait times and improved patient experience without increasing staff workload.
  • AI-Assisted Referrals: Transcribes a referral request (via fax) to provide the required information for manual review. Benefits include a faster referral process and quicker access to specialty care. Over a 5-month period, Phreesia claimed to achieve a 56% increase in monthly referral volume for its provider customers.
  • Out-of-Network Tags: Identifies out-of-network (OON) patients. Benefits include streamlining the insurance validation process, reducing claim denials, and reducing uncompensated care and last-minute insurance-related cancellations. Phreesia claims it can save its customers up to eight days of manual verification per month.

The constant flow of new products and services on top of the existing robust product and service ecosystem gives Phreesia the ability to win new customers and expand within current customers.

Phreesia also provided an update (the second annual evaluation) to its Kidney Care Choices Model, a partnership in place with the Center for Medicare and Medicaid Innovation (CMMI) since 2019, now extended through August, 2026. The partnership targets improving the care and quality of life for patients with advanced chronic kidney disease (CKD) and end-stage renal disease (ESRD). This work aligns with Phreesia's vision of "every patient being an active participant in their care." The Kidney Care Choices Model achieved encouraging wins across a range of metrics (quoted from the shareholder letter):

  • Clinically meaningful increases in PAM scores—an average of 8 points—across patient populations, with the highest increase among transplant patients. (In previous studies, changes of this magnitude have been associated with an 8% reduction in healthcare costs.
  • Significant improvements in other targeted outcomes, including:
    • 31% increase in optimal ESRD starts
    • 40% increase in preemptive waitlisting
    • 69% increase in preemptive transplants and 22% increase in living donor transplants
    • 10% increase in home dialysis
    • 22% increase in home dialysis training

These kinds of wins boost my confidence in investing in Phreesia as an established participant in efforts to improve the healthcare system and experience.

Guidance

Improved guidance for fiscal year 2026 did not deliver the typical support for the stock, but the guidance gives me another reason to look for my next entry point to add shares. Phreesia shifted the range of its adjusted EBITDA guidance higher by $2M to $87M to $92M. This is the second bump higher in EBITDA guidance for 2026. The company reiterated its revenue guidance of $472M to $482M and AHSCs (average healthcare services clients) at 4,500 (a 3.7% year-over-year growth rate). The company will update its guidance further after the AccessOne deal closes in Q3 or Q4.

This guidance translates into 12% to 15% year-over-year revenue growth. This will be the 5th year in a row of declining growth. Annual revenue growth for FY 2025 was 18%. The expected 7% year-over-year growth rate for AHSC is also a notable stepdown from the prior year's 17% year-over-year growth rate. This slowing growth trajectory means that Phreesia needs to soon demonstrate stabilizing customer and revenue growth. The slowing growth is the biggest risk to my investment thesis. I am currently assuming that the company's business momentum will soon stabilize the growth rate, with the AccessOne acquisition contributing to that stabilization. The company's bold TAM-sizing also suggests that stabilizing growth is a realistic expectation.

Comparisons and Competition

As long as Phreesia can maintain double-digit revenue growth and continue to improve its free cash flow margin, then its low valuation makes holding the stock more palatable. PHR trades around 3.4 times sales, trailing and forward. Yet, valuation is the lowest grade that contributes to the buy rating for the Seeking Alpha quant model. The quant model rates PHR most favorably for its growth and earnings revisions. A software stock with double-digit growth and improving cash flow margin should easily have at least a 5.0 price/sales ratio. Thus, I see almost 50% upside potential for PHR in the most optimistic scenario (for example, with the removal of post-acquisition overhang) in a year or two.

PHR also compares favorably to its peers and competitors. The stock is now outperforming its peer group (I added Doximity (DOX) to the Seeking Alpha peer group) as the only stock in this universe clinging to a year-to-date gain. Yet, PHR’s 0.5% year-to-date return now underperforms the 8.3% year-to-date performance of the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM). Note that Schrodinger (SDGR) has a price/sales ratio of 5.5 and an expected 12.5% year-over-year expected revenue growth rate, thus making a 5.0 price/sales ratio target for PHR achievable with stabilizing growth.

The Trade

With PHR once again trading below the breakout line I have watched all year, I plan to make my next buy after PHR recovers 200 DMA support (as referenced in the intro). I continue to expect in due time that PHR will not just break out above this trend line but also hold the breakout. The combination of non-cyclical growth and low valuation should make PHR increasingly attractive to the market despite any initial disappointments over the size and cost of the acquisition or the caveated net income line.

data.title: Fresh Market Highs, Here's What I See
data.description:
Introduction

The headlines ran bullish as we crossed the 46,000 level on the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI). It's not just the Dow; the S&P 500 Index (SP500), NASDAQ Composite Index (COMP:IND), and market darling group, the Magnificent Seven, are all also at all-time highs after rebounding from April lows and continuing the "most hated V-shaped rally (0:15)," which appears to be all gas, no brakes, as the kids say.

It's times like these I feel like grounding is important for investors, as irrational exuberance plays out around us.

Look at just how far the Dow has come in the last century (95 years, I can only go back to 1930). This really puts into perspective the explosive growth of the market post-GFC. The growth has been so steep in the last 15 years.

It's easy looking at that chart to say that the answer is to always hold on tight. It would be a cheap shot to show a picture of the NIKKEI 225 Index (NKY:IND) from 1989-2023, where they had lost decades, plural. Current America is not 1990s Japan, and I won't pretend that it's anywhere close.

However, there are a few major risks to consider for investors in these broad market indexes that I think we should consider here.

Valuation Risk

The implication of this market being at all-time highs is also that valuations are at all-time highs. Beware that in past market conditions when valuations were this high, the following years usually had lower returns than expected and definitely lower than the previous years that led up to the valuation peak.

Sometimes, crashes follow. Other times, it's just long periods of low market performance as earnings catch up to valuations. Those periods seem less common and shorter in our modern era.

I'm not prescribing a crash or some major correction here, for the record. I'm bullish on the market, still sticking to my call back in June; I think we could go another 10% higher on the Nasdaq by the end of the year, which would bring the S&P and Dow along with it.

Knowing that we are in a bubble is different from knowing when it will pop.

All that is to say that we should be expecting the unexpected from here and that being prudent matters most in times like this when valuations are frothy. When valuations crater, it's usually not due to a surge in earnings justifying them. It's usually due to a cratering share price.

Even though we are at all-time highs in the market and at relative highs in valuation, we are still below the earnings per share highs we saw in 2022. This is not as big a deal as it may seem but it is worth noting as we enter an economy that is growing more from AI CapEx than consumption.

Median earnings for U.S. large caps are still trending in the right direction, part of why I am still bullish, but this chart is telling of the market conditions in a lot of ways.

Concentration Risk

I've hammered this point before, but it bears mentioning again: the S&P 500 (SPX) and other major indexes like the NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX) have a concentration problem where they are incredibly top-heavy. The top ten stocks make up over 50% of the Nasdaq 100, with its top stock, NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), taking up nearly 10%.

The Dow Jones has a different problem, because while it is more naturally concentrated due to its holding of 30 stocks, it uses a price-weighting scheme that makes it top-heavy as well, but in a different way. Its largest holding is The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) at over 10%.

To be fair to the Magnificent Seven, stocks that are the cause of a lot of this movement, you can see their clear correlation with all of these market indexes, even the Dow. You can sort the indexes by concentration of mega-cap tech, high to low (in our case, top to bottom of this next chart), and see the same performance patterns in all of them, but less and less pronounced—read as "volatile."

When you sort the S&P 500 by holdings, going from most to least concentrated, you get the same effect. This timeline is shorter due to the limited lifespan of the top 20 ETFs, TOPT.

Economic & Fed Risk

Warning: I'm going to be a bit of a wonk and link a lot of data here so I can keep this short but deep and allow folks to go deeper if they want.

On the note of economic risk, I think we are in overall decent economic shape given we see central bank rates come down as they are expected to over the next few months, and over a longer timeframe, inflation is still running a little hot and is getting hotter, but unemployment is still below its historical average, despite weakening labor data and heavy revisions.

This confluence has put the Federal Reserve in a monetary policy "rock and hard place" with its Federal Funds Rate, which drives prevailing interest rates, and it has been looking like they will begin another cutting cycle and, over the long term, will keep up quantitative easing practices as a "default" policy mode even if it means accepting higher inflation levels.

All the while, the U.S. Treasury is looking to expand the national debt by another $1.6T in H2 2025, continually pushing more money into the system as they borrow to spend while cutting revenue from the largest sources (income and corporate taxes).

At the simplest level where it is still useful, monetary and fiscal policy are aligning in such a way that we should expect an increase in overall liquidity, which is beneficial for asset prices.

We commonly measure liquidity with money supply, or M2.

A derailing of this narrative, say if we have a Fed that begins to call for rate hikes, would be a risk that investors need to watch out for. The market is currently pricing in a very dovish Fed, and if they don't get it, they may cause a scene. A hawkish Fed could cause further contraction of M2, which would in turn pull asset prices with it.

What I'm Doing About It

The answer to these concerns is the same: manage your risk. That looks different for different investors — one of the beauties of DIY investing.

While some investors use options; others prefer to manage risk via position sizing, which is my favorite to advocate and use, personally and professionally.

Make sure not to use "position sizing" as a way to push yourself entirely out of the market. Even at all-time highs, there is a chance we go higher. Regardless of all the warnings I can give, prices can go up for any reason, and they often do in the short term. The data I'm pointing to here is all long-term, and so it's important to stay invested for the long term, even if your positions are kept small to ensure that your risk tolerance is met.

I am considering trimming back my equity positions from where they are now, nearly fully deployed, but my optimistic view of the market's technicals has me staying in for now. I am positioned well, however, and have not overexposed myself to the market if there is a deep pullback or a correction to valuations.

However, as I have been writing about, I believe it is in investors' best interests to consider markets outside U.S. large caps, which are not at all-time highs and could have a significant comeback. If anything, the expected returns are higher due to buying in at lower valuations. While cheap can stay cheap forever, and there are great arguments for American exceptionalism, the charts don't lie. Sometimes, betting on small caps and ex-U.S. stocks is the way to go.

I think we may be entering a phase like that over the next few years. That will be a longer-term trend and may take even longer to play out, depending on how long the AI party continues in full swing.

That party is still on in the U.S. for now, and I will not be reducing my U.S. exposure, but I am adding to other positions currently, and I think it's very prudent to consider the stocks left behind as we reach these fresh all-time highs.

Conclusion

With the major indexes hitting new highs, I believe it's a good moment for investors to ground themselves and be reminded of the major risks still at play in this market, like valuations running away from earnings (ex-Mag 7), index concentration in the top firms, and looming economic risks dependent on monetary and fiscal policy decisions out of the hands of investors.

The best way to deal with it is twofold:

  1. Reevaluate your risk tolerance, making sure that you are positioned so that a deep, prolonged correction wouldn't force you to sell, either from a solvency or psychological standpoint.
  2. Consider opportunities to diversify when U.S. markets are at all-time highs, especially for investors with portfolios heavy on U.S. large-cap stocks.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Nvidia: Undervalued At Current Levels, Poised For Multi-Year Growth
data.description:
Some investors may look at the title of this article and think that after being up 31.95% YTD and 51.48% over the past year, there is no way shares of NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) can break out again. The bear case has revolved around the amount of capital being allocated toward CapEx eventually slowing, causing sales in GPUs to plateau or decline. Eventually I do believe this may occur, and while my initial bull case for NVDA had an expanding CapEx allocation period of 18-24 months, I think that there is still a multi-year buildout ahead of us. I read through all the main technology earnings reports, and I do not see any indication that CapEx is slowing or proprietary chips will impact the demand for NVDA’s GPUs. Outside of the hyperscalers Oracle Corporation (ORCL) and CoreWeave (CRWV) released critical information that supports an extended CapEx buildout that includes NVDA’s GPUs. On ORCL’s recent earnings call, Safra Catz (ORCL CEO) indicated that their remaining performance obligations reached $455 billion which was an increase of 359% YoY and they are expecting to allocate $35 billion toward CapEx this fiscal year. CoreWeave reported that its revenue backlog increased by 85% to $30.1 billion in Q2 2025. I believe that NVDA delivered one of the best earnings reports I have ever read, and while its shares have generated enormous returns for long-term shareholders, I believe they still have the ability to breakout well past $200.

Following up on my previous article about NVIDIA Corporation

At the beginning of June, I wrote an article about NVDA (can be read here) where I discussed how its explosive growth could exceed the market's expectations. Since then shares of NVDA have appreciated by 24.32% while the S&P 500 has gained 10.03%. I felt that NVDA’s free cash flow (FCF) growth and massive share buybacks were being looked over by the market as their ash generation and strategic initiatives positioned NVDA to outperform. NVDA’s expansion into sovereign AI, enterprise AI, and robotics, plus the Blackwell platform ramp, significantly expanded its total addressable market, which was bullish for its top-line revenue growth. NVDA just delivered a blowout quarter with some of the best results I have ever seen from a company. I am following up with a new article to discuss why I am more bullish than ever for NVDA and believe shares will breakout well past the $200 level.

NVIDIA Corporation delivered a blowout quarter and the results are very bullish

I have never seen the amount of growth NVDA has accomplished in such a short period of time. NVDA went from generating $26.91 billion in revenue during 2021 and $26.97 billion during 2022 to growing their revenue by 125.85% ($33.95 billion) in 2023 to $60.92 billion and then by another 114.20% ($69.58 billion) in 2024 to $130.5 billion. After the first 2 quarters in 2025, NVDA has already increased its trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue by 26.61% ($34.72 billion) to $165.22 billion, and there is a good chance that they finish the year having generated around $200 billion, which would be an increase of 53.26% ($69.5 billion) YoY. For me, the story isn’t just about the revenue growth as NVDA’s profitability is growing by massive amounts and their margins are off the charts for a semiconductor company. Last year NVDA generated $97.86 billion in gross profit for a margin of 74.99%. NVDA’s operating income came in at $81.45 billion for a margin of 62.42%, and its net income was $72.88 billion, which was a 55.85% profit margin. In the TTM, NVDA has generated $115.4 billion in gross profit for a margin of 69.85%. Its operating income is $95.98 billion, and its net income is $86.6 billion, which are margins of 58.09% and 52.41%. The fact that NVDA has a profit margin that exceeds 50% with this type of growth at this scale is incredible, and no other company in the Magnificent Seven can match this.

When I look at NVDA on a quarterly basis, the numbers from Q2 are incredible. NVDA generated $46.74 billion in revenue, which was an increase of 55.6% ($16.70 billion) YoY and 6.08% ($2.68 billion) QoQ. NVDA’s gross profit increased 49.96% ($11.28 billion) YoY and 26.94% ($7.19 billion) QoQ to $33.85 billion. In Q2, NVDA’s gross profit margin was 72.42%, which paved the way for some of its highest operating and net income margins. NVDA was able to grow its operating income by 52.56% ($9.8 billion) YoY and 31.44% ($6.8 billion) QoQ to $28.44 billion. NVDA’s net income grew 59.18% ($9.82 billion) YoY and 40.73% ($7.65 billion) QoQ to $26.42 billion. NVDA’s operating and profit margins were 60.84% and 56.53% in Q2. The fact that NVDA operated at a margin above 60% and netted over 55% on the bottom line is unlike anything I have seen from a company that brought in over $45 billion in revenue over a 3-month period. This type of growth is making their forward estimates look inexpensive.

When I look at the EPS projections for NVDA and the rest of the Magnificent Seven from the analyst community, shares of NVDA look very undervalued. I also added Costco Wholesale (COST) in my valuation table to emphasize how undervalued NVDA is. NVDA is expected to generate $4.49 of EPS in 2025 and trades at 39.46 times 2025 earnings. Over the next 2-years the analyst community expects EPS to grow its EPS by 67.26% ($3.02) to $7.51. NVDA is trading at 23.11 times 2027 earnings with almost 70% growth expected over the next 2 fiscal years, while Apple (AAPL) is trading at 26.20 times 2027 earnings with less than 20% growth on the horizon. COST on the other hand generated $268.78 billion in revenue during the TTM and has a profit margin of 2.92%, as their net income was $7.84 billion, yet it trades at 53.16 times 2025 earnings. COST is expected to generate 22.49% of EPS growth over the next 2-years and trades at 43.40 times 2027 earnings. When I look at the amount of growth that NVDA is projected to generate, its current profitability and margins, the current valuation looks very inexpensive, especially in an environment where the CapEx spend isn’t deteriorating.

The combination of AMZN, MSFT, META, and GOOGL allocated $88.25 billion toward CapEx in Q2 2025. This was an increase of 66.97% ($35.39 billion) YoY. These companies have allocated $291.35 billion toward CapEx in the TTM and their not stopping any time soon. When I look at the quarterly consolidated totals, the growth rate is actually increasing. In Q2 2024 the combination of these 4 companies increased their CapEx spend QoQ by 19.33% ($8.56 billion) to $52.85 billion. In Q2 2025, the QoQ increase from them was 22.73% ($16.34 billion) to $88.25 billion. These companies are spending more on CapEx and now companies like ORCL are trying to buy up as many GPUs as they can. I am not seeing any indication that allocations toward CapEx is slowing down and when I read all the earnings reports each of these companies is committing more capital toward CapEx. This makes me believe that the analyst community may be conservative in their estimates and shares of NVDA could be very undervalued, as they could outperform the massive growth that is already expected.

NVIDIA Corporation is positioned for growth and I think shares are undervalued

NVDA’s Q2 results showcased their exceptional operational performance and strategic advancements across its business segments. Data Centers remained NVDA’s growth engine as it generated $41.1 billion in revenue which was an increase of 56% YoY and 5% QoQ. The Blackwell Data Center revenue increased 17% QoQ as the production of Blackwell Ultra recognized high demand. NVDA’s GB300 platform began production shipments in Q2 as its factory conversions supported its ramp to approximately 1,000 racks per week. NVDA is expected to expand the amount of racks per week it generates in Q3. NVDA also announced that its RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition GPU is being adopted by global enterprises, which include Disney (DIS), Eli Lilly (LLY), and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM). NVDA collaborated with France, Germany, Italy, Spain, and the U.K. to build Blackwell AI infrastructure, which included the world’s first industrial A.I. cloud for European manufacturers. I had indicated in my previous article that Sovereign AI would be important for NVDA, and we are seeing significant progress in this area. NVDA was able to help optimize sovereign large language models with NVIDIA Nemotron that were available on Perplexity.

One of the most important things to come out of Q2 was that NVDA is forecasting that there will be a $3-$4 trillion A.I. infrastructure market by the end of the decade which is based on the rapidly escalating annual A.I. investments. NVDA believes that the combination of AMZN, META, MSFT, and GOOGL will spend $600 billion annually in the next 2-years. I think that NVDA is positioned for sustained growth that will last at least 3-5 years, maybe longer. NVDA has become an A.I. infrastructure company that provides full-stack systems which integrates chips, networking, systems, software, and algorithms. I believe that this is what the skeptics are overlooking as NVDA has expanded its operations beyond chips to generate recurring revenue streams. We are also still in the early stages of full self-driving, humanoid robotics, and automated factories/warehouses which will all have continuous innovation and increased demand. I think that as the next several years unfold a larger emphasis will be put on their software leadership as it will generate more revenue through platforms like CUDA, TensorRT-LLM, and Dynamo as they are already being used by more than 6 million developers. NVDA has a large opportunity to generate recurring revenue as they become more entrenched throughout the A.I. ecosystem.

Risks to investing in NVIDIA Corporation

While I am very bullish on NVDA there are risk factors that investors should consider before allocating capital toward it. A large portion of their revenue is comprised of 4 major companies which include Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), and Microsoft (MSFT). If these companies have breakthroughs in their proprietary chips than can offset the amount of GPU’s needed from NVDA it could allow them to allocate less capital toward CapEx. If this happens NVDA’s revenue and profitability could come under pressure as their forward growth is tied to GPU sales. The other large risk is that software gets so advanced that companies won’t need as many GPUs to train their models which could also hurt NVDAs top and bottom line. At some point the demand for NVDA’s chips will become cyclical the only question is when does that happen. Investors are currently frontrunning when the shift will happen and there could be less time than I believe as to when this occurs. Investors should do their own due diligence before allocating capital toward NVDA.

Conclusion

I still believe NVDA is one of the largest opportunities in the market despite being a $4.3 trillion company. The reality is that over the TTM NVDA has generated $86.6 billion in net income and is trending to produce over $100 billion in profitability if their margins and growth remains sustainable. NVDA just approved an additional $60 billion of buybacks on top of the $14.7 billion they had left on the previous authorization. As NVDA becomes more profitable they will likely reward shareholders through larger buybacks which will help them scale EPS. Based on what I see happening from the hyperscalers and other technology companies in their CapEx allocations I think that NVDA has fortified their operations with a multi-year roadmap that keeps their customers trapped within their ecosystem. None of the hyperscalers can take their foot off the gas when it comes to their dependence on NVDA’s chips and services otherwise they run the risk of falling behind their competitors if their wrong. NVDA is expected to generate around $54 billion of revenue with a GAAP gross profit margin of 73.3% compared to 72.42% in Q2. This means that NVDA could exceed $30 billion of profitability in Q3 and I am expecting that if this happens the analyst community will raise their forward EPS estimates and shares of NVDA will breakout to the upside well past $200.

data.title: Core & Main: The Growth Narrative Is Under Huge Pressure (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Overview

I downgraded to a hold rating for Core & Main, Inc. (NYSE:CNM), as I did not like how the macroeconomy was moving; coupled with the weak guidance, I believed the stock would not see any positive traction. While the share price has risen since March, I believe my rating was right, as the stock plummeted right after the 2Q25 earnings and is now back to near March's share price level. This 2Q25 quarter showed it had reinforced the bear case. With residential demand collapsing and internal execution missteps, I believe CNM's growth story is breaking down. I downgrade to a sell rating.

Poor 2Q25 Results

Unfortunately, CNM's 2Q25 results have made me even more bearish. Net sales saw $2.093 billion, which was a 6.6% y/y growth on a reported basis, but organic growth was only ~5%. Growth was also not broad-based. While pipes and storm drainage grew, meter product sales declined y/y. End-market performance told a similar story. Municipal demand remained stable, and non-residential held up, but weakness in residential lot development overshadowed these positives. Given that lot development is a forward indicator of future activity, this trend is very concerning. Even more troubling, profitability deteriorated despite gross margin improvement. Gross margin expanded 40 bps to 26.8%, but this was entirely erased by a lack of cost discipline. SG&A grew 12.7% y/y to $302 million, nearly double the rate of sales growth, pushing SG&A to 14.4% of sales versus 13.6% in 2Q24. This drove adj. EBITDA margin down 40 bps to 12.7%, with adj. EBITDA up only 3.5% y/y.

The Bear Thesis

I believe the bear case for CNM is playing out in real time. At a high level, CNM appears to be in the middle of a perfect storm, where an important end market is collapsing at the very same moment internal costs are getting out of control. This is going to hit both the top and bottom lines simultaneously, and I don't expect the market to take this positively.

The most urgent issue is the continued softness in residential lot development, which represents ~20% of CNM's sales. In just one quarter, management's outlook has changed violently from "Flat" to "Down Low Double Digits" for FY25. This is a massive downgrade, and I don't think this represents just a routine adjustment. Management's commentary only made this concern sound even worse. Specifically, they noted projects in key markets are not just being scaled back but, in many cases, stopped entirely. Viewed from another angle, there is a huge hole in CNM's revenue base (20%) that is going to heavily weigh on performance.

Like I have noted above, there was a clear lack of cost discipline, and I think the really negative takeaway here is that this elevated cost structure is likely to stay for the near term. Management attributed the increase to everything from rising employee benefit costs to integration challenges from acquisitions, but whatever the drivers are, the reality is that this cost surge is going to eat into profitability. I don't think the announced "cost-out actions" will do anything to instill confidence. Management itself admitted the bulk of savings won't be realized until FY26, which basically means 2H25 will face the same pressure.

Aside from these, CNM is also showing terrible execution missteps that add to the bear case, most notably in meter products, where sales fell 7% y/y due to project delays. This misstep is damaging because it shows CNM problems are not just due to the macro-sensitive residential market but also within its own operational execution. Management pointed to a difficult 2Q24 comparison (saw 48% growth in the category), but it doesn't change the fact that CNM failed to manage customer timelines or deliver on schedule.

Put together, the end result is that management was forced to cut FY25 adj. EBITDA guidance, confirming that margins will contract in FY25. In my view, this is more than just a missed quarter; I think it has put a huge dent in the prior growth narrative and is a serious hit to management credibility.

Valuation

CNM valuation has seen a sharp reset from low-20x pre-results back to its 10-year average of 18x forward PE. I think there are more downsides to come from here. Consensus is still expecting EPS growth in the high teens for FY26 and low teens in FY27. I truly wonder if that will come true if 20% of sales are effectively coming to a halt and costs are growing. If what management said is right, and the US housing market continues to be in a slump, I see a good chance for CNM to miss its own guidance and consensus estimates. This should trigger waves of estimate downgrades, further driving multiples downwards.

Risk

If CNM is able to consistently expand gross margins through pricing and sourcing initiatives, it may offset the SG&A pressure more effectively than I expected. Furthermore, the resilient municipal market, supported by infrastructure spending and a stable non-residential sector, could prove strong enough to better cushion the sharp decline in residential demand.

Conclusion

I give a sell rating for CNM. The collapse in residential lot development, increasing SG&A burden, and poor execution are negative for both growth and profitability. Management's cut to FY25 EBITDA guidance highlights that this is more than just a soft quarter but a reset of expectations. With consensus estimates still overly optimistic and valuation likely to compress further, I see more downside risk ahead.
